title: Synesthesia
phenomenon: An inducer in one domain reliably and involuntarily evokes a conscious concurrent in another, such as a grapheme evoking a colour not present in the stimulus.
adversarialPrompt: Explain stable, involuntary cross-domain conscious concurrents in synesthesia. Distinguish the experienced concurrent from learned association, imagery, attention, memory, and report strategy.
rationale: A strong account must locate both the inducer and the experienced concurrent while explaining how content absent from the external stimulus becomes consciously present.
assumptions: The reported concurrent is experienced rather than strategically described.
Inducer-concurrent pairings are sufficiently stable to test.
knownConfounds: Learned associations can mimic stable inducer-concurrent mappings.
Imagery ability and attention can influence behavioural signatures.
Self-selection can distort prevalence and strength estimates.
targetCommitments: How internally generated content becomes consciously present.
Why the concurrent is stable, automatic, and stimulus-specific.
What separates a conscious concurrent from association or imagery.
changeMindObservation: The theory's proposed conscious mechanism can be disrupted without changing preregistered measures that distinguish concurrents from learned association or imagery.
